citations.fact: The U.S. National Cislunar Science & Technology Strategy defines cislunar space as the “three-dimensional volume of space beyond Earth’s geosynchronous orbit” primarily under Earth/Moon gravity, explicitly including Earth–Moon Lagrange regions, trajectories using those regions, and the lunar surface.
The strategy notes cislunar volume is >2,000× the volume inside GEO.
The distance scale reaches lunar distances (~384,400 km average Earth–Moon distance), which drives low signal-to-noise sensing, long custody gaps, and slower update rates than in LEO.

Foundational U.S. policy (Space Policy Directive-3) defines SSA as “knowledge and characterization of space objects and their operational environment” and STM as planning/coordination/synchronization to enhance safety, stability, and sustainability.
The cislunar strategy explicitly ties SSA to collision avoidance, RPO support, identification of anomalous or potentially dangerous operations, and even planetary-defense synergy.
A cislunar SSA paper notes that in the cislunar realm accelerations are not directed toward a single central body and that OD methods must be “significantly adapted.”
NASA’s modeling reference flags that TLEs assume simplified force models and are “mainly limited to Earth orbiting” applications, motivating higher-fidelity ephemeris-based propagation.

A cislunar SSA study finds that even with limiting magnitude ~20, large fractions of selected cislunar orbits can be unobservable due to geometry and background moonlight; it concludes a global electro-optical network is mandatory but still may be insufficient in some orbits.

Purpose-built cislunar sensing concepts (e.g., AFRL’s Oracle family and CHPS) are explicitly motivated by Earth-based exclusion zones and >10× GEO distance scaling, and implement wide-/narrow-FOV sensors plus onboard processing to sustain custody and reduce downlink burden.
NASA’s DSN radiometric tracking (range/Doppler/VLBI-family observables) remains the gold standard for cooperative targets, but is capacity constrained and scheduled.
Cislunar uncertainty “structure is far more complex,” and can form coils/crossovers over days.

Mature near-Earth conjunction products and standards (CCSDS TDM for tracking data exchange; CCSDS CDM for conjunction exchange) can be reused while adjusting thresholds and screening volumes to cislunar geometry/uncertainty.

Space-Track’s Spaceflight Safety Handbook provides an example of how Deep Space screening/notification criteria are structured (e.g., miss-distance-based criteria for “deep space”; Pc-based criteria for LEO), offering a template for “tiered” cislunar alerting.
Space weather can degrade RF propagation and navigation accuracy via ionospheric disturbance, demanding monitoring/mitigation (NOAA SWPC).
Cyber resiliency must be treated as mission-critical infrastructure per SPD-5 cybersecurity principles for space systems.

This report is limited to public/unclassified capabilities and standards; budgets, legal/regulatory posture, and access to classified sensors and tasking authorities are unspecified, so recommendations emphasize scalable architectures and interoperability rather than force-structure specifics.

A U.S. interagency definition in the National Cislunar S&T Strategy defines cislunar as the volume beyond GEO mainly under Earth/Moon gravity, including Earth–Moon Lagrange regions, trajectories using those regions, and the lunar surface.

A NASA cislunar “destination” analysis defined the “cis-lunar domain” more broadly as deep space under Earth–Moon gravitational influence, including Earth-centered orbits (LEO→GEO→HEO), Earth–Moon libration points (EML1–EML5), and low lunar orbit (LLO).
An “Earth-proximal deep space” catalog (GEO/HEO/hyper-GEO) can leverage mature Earth-orbit conventions and sensors (e.g., DARC for GEO all-weather coverage).

U.S. STM policy (SPD-3) frames SSA/STM around safety, stability, and sustainability, and explicitly calls for continuously updated catalogs, automated processes for collision avoidance, and timely actionable conjunction assessments.
The cislunar S&T strategy explicitly cites collision avoidance, RPO support, and anomalous operation identification as SSA use cases.

Characterization and attribution in practice blends photometric/IR signatures, RF identification, and behavior (“patterns of life”), consistent with broader SDA doctrine evolution beyond simple tracking.

Anomaly detection and intent assessment involves detecting unexpected maneuvers, station-keeping failures, tumbling, breakups, and deviations from declared plans; it requires prioritizing follow-up tasking and analyst review.
SPD-3 emphasizes SSA data interoperability, data sharing, and standards development.
NASA describes Gateway’s NRHO as a “halo orbit” around the Moon used because it offers favorable access and stability characteristics relative to other lunar orbits.

Smallsats and CubeSats are increasingly common around the Moon; they often have higher area-to-mass ratios and constrained comm/nav, which increases uncertainty growth and maneuver detectability challenges.
The high A/M of debris and fragmentation products can make solar radiation pressure (SRP) dominate error growth far from Earth.
The cislunar S&T strategy explicitly links expanded SSA to benefits for detecting and warning of potentially hazardous asteroids.

A rigorous cislunar SSA study notes that, away from the near-lunar region, the environment contains “small accelerations” not directed toward a single central body; the absence of GPS-like infrastructure and increased distances also stress operations.

The restricted three-body problem (CR3BP) is useful for orbit-family reasoning, but high-fidelity operations generally require ephemeris-based numerical propagation (Earth/Moon/Sun and often additional bodies) and careful frame/time conventions.
In cislunar regimes, uncertainty can develop complex “coil” and “crossover” structures over days, weakening linear covariance mapping assumptions.

A cislunar SA system should anticipate custody breaks (periods where no sensor can observe the target because of geometry, exclusion zones, or weather), and treat prediction uncertainty growth as a first-class scheduling driver rather than a secondary artifact.
In cislunar, sensitivity is often the primary discriminator because range is extreme.
GEODSS uses 1 m telescopes and is referred to as “Deep STARE”.
Large DoD optics at Maui (AEOS 3.6 m) are used as an example system.
GEODSS “sees” objects far dimmer than human eye.
DARC (planned) provides 24/7 all-weather deep-space detection/tracking/characterization for GEO coverage.
Radar performance falls rapidly with range (physics + power); designs are focused on GEO belt coverage rather than full cislunar volume.
Space Fence (S-band phased array) primarily operates in LEO; it also supports GEO cueing.
Space Fence provides high-cadence uncued detection in LEO and potential cue support to higher regimes.
Space Fence is not designed for lunar-distance objects; its energy and geometry limit reach into true cislunar.
SBSS provides 24/7 metric and SOI (Space Object Identification) without weather/daylight/atmosphere limits.
GSSAP offers a near-GEO vantage and RPO-enabled characterization.
Space-based EO sensors eliminate weather/atmospheric distortion constraints and offer unique geometry for characterization and anomaly resolution.
Space-based EO sensors (Earth-orbit SDA) are still largely GEO-centric; they do not automatically solve solar exclusion for cislunar transfers and are limited in numbers.
The AFRL CHPS concept is designed to detect/track/identify objects at lunar distance (~385,000 km).
The Oracle family sensors (wide + narrow FOV) use onboard processing and are placed in the cislunar region to reduce exclusion.
Cislunar-focused space-based EO sensors are specifically engineered to overcome Earth-view exclusion zones and extreme ranges; they explicitly address comm/nav challenges via onboard capability.
Cislunar-focused space-based EO sensors are limited by spacecraft resources; communication at lunar distances is harder; and they still require accurate self-navigation.
NASA DSN provides range, Doppler, and VLBI-family techniques for cooperative radiometric tracking.
ESA ESTRACK provides TT&C links for deep space missions.
Delta-DOR is explicitly used to precisely locate spacecraft.
Cooperative radiometric tracking requires a cooperative transponder and scheduling; DSN capacity constraints motivate autonomous supplements.
ILRS provides SLR/LLR data and derived products.
Very high range precision (cm→sub-cm) is demonstrated in modern laser ranging systems.
Laser ranging requires retroreflectors/cooperative geometry, is affected by weather, and has tasking complexity.
CAPSTONE demonstrates cislunar spacecraft-to-spacecraft navigation crosslinks; the cislunar strategy targets cislunar comm/PNT infrastructure.
RF sensing/crosslinks reduce dependence on Earth-based tracking and enable local PNT-like behavior in cislunar.
RF sensing/crosslinks require compatible radios/protocols and cooperative participation; they also have spectrum and security concerns.

Optical astrometric accuracies of ~1 arcsecond are commonly cited for good optical tracking pipelines, while some operational systems historically were closer to several arcseconds (e.g., GEODSS accuracy discussed as ~4 arcseconds post-upgrade in one assessment).
Cislunar custody typically relies on multiple tracklets, diverse geometry, and/or range-bearing observables (radiometric or laser) for high-confidence screening and maneuver detection.
Ground-only EO + limited radar offers mature operations; it is scalable via more telescopes and good for photometric SOI.
Ground-only EO + limited radar leads to custody breaks and exclusion zones; it requires a global network and still may miss large orbit fractions in some cislunar families.
Ground EO + GEO-focused radar (e.g., DARC) offers 24/7 all-weather capabilities that complement optical limitations; it provides improved sensitivity/accuracy versus legacy GEO radars.
Ground EO + GEO-focused radar (e.g., DARC) is still not a “full cislunar” solution; radar physics limits its range to lunar distances for small targets.
Ground EO + Earth-orbit space-based EO (SBSS/GSSAP class) provides an unobstructed view without weather/time-of-day/atmosphere; it offers RPO-enabled characterization in the GEO region.
Ground EO + Earth-orbit space-based EO (SBSS/GSSAP class) is asset-limited; its geometry is still Earth-centric; and it is not purpose-built to defeat cislunar exclusion zones.
Ground EO + cislunar space-based EO (Oracle/CHPS class) is purpose-built for >10× GEO distances and cislunar exclusion-zone geometry; its onboard processing reduces downlink load.
Ground EO + cislunar space-based EO (Oracle/CHPS class) requires deep-space comm/nav; it has higher development/ops complexity and initially small constellation numbers.
DSN-style range/Doppler/VLBI observables are designed for deep-space navigation and are directly applicable to lunar-distance OD.
Ground EO provides independent custody and characterization, but may face orbit-dependent unobservable arcs.
Oracle is explicitly designed with wide- and narrow-field sensors to discover and maintain custody, and uses onboard processing to reduce downlink volume and latency pressure.
The cislunar strategy highlights the need to “optimize terrestrial, Lunar-based, and space-based sensors” and to support standardized data formats and exchanges.
DARC is designed to supplement optical limitations and provide global GEO coverage.

Key interoperability standards that support this flow include CCSDS Tracking Data Messages (TDM) for tracking observables and CCSDS Conjunction Data Messages (CDM) for conjunction information exchange.
NASA’s cislunar modeling reference provides explicit SRP modeling equations and notes that eclipse factors are epoch-dependent and nontrivial.
Simplified dynamics can lead to trajectories “very far from the truth” in cislunar contexts (as shown in cislunar SSA research comparing CR3BP with full ephemerides).

TLEs assume a simplified force model (limited zonal harmonics plus simplified drag/third-body/SRP) and therefore are mainly limited to Earth-orbit applications; extrapolating them to cislunar custody is not reliable.
A cislunar SSA study shows uncertainty “coils” and other complex structures and argues existing near-Earth approaches must be reevaluated.

Periodic nonlinear validation (unscented transform sampling or Monte Carlo ensembles) is important for high-consequence intervals, such as expected maneuvers, close approaches, or custody-loss windows.
Per-sensor astrometric residual targets: ~1 arcsec is achievable with good pipelines; legacy/operational systems may be several arcsec.

For initial detection, tune false-alarm control to maintain operator workload; prioritize repeated detections across frames over single-frame “hits” in high-background conditions (moonlight/stray light).
Start with gate sizes tied to predicted covariance; enlarge gates for cislunar because uncertainty growth can be non-intuitive and may “coil.”
When the dynamic model is strongly non-Keplerian, prefer ephemeris-informed IOD approaches (recent cislunar work focuses on ephemeris-derived models).
Batch LS is standard in OD toolchains (GMAT and Orekit both implement it).
A cislunar horizon-based navigation study explicitly feeds optical measurements to an EKF in simulation, illustrating the workflow concept.
Public references indicate ~1 arcsec is achievable with proper processing; GEODSS accuracy has been discussed as ~4 arcsec in a historical assessment.
SLR systems can reach sub-centimeter accuracy in laser ranging.
The cislunar SSA literature shows Gaussian validity can degrade over 10–30 days; for short-term (minutes–days) it may still degrade depending on the orbit family and geometry.
In cislunar, SRP modeling uncertainty and eclipses are significant, so ensure the “maneuver hypothesis” competes with “model mismatch” hypotheses.

Pc computation is typically performed in the collision plane; Space-Track’s handbook describes this 2D reduction approach and notes covariance scaling may be needed if uncertainties are underestimated.

The Space-Track handbook’s “concern list” criteria show Pc ≥ e^-7 for Near Earth and miss distance ≤ 5 km for “Deep Space” style regimes—useful as an initial framework conceptually, but must be re-derived for cislunar regimes.
SPD-3 emphasizes that collision avoidance services require continuously updated catalogs and automated processes.
SPD-3 explicitly prioritizes interoperability and data sharing under national security constraints.
The NASA cislunar modeling reference emphasizes consistent reference frames and time standards (e.g., WGS84 for Earth-fixed; a celestial reference frame for space; UTC time).
Recent cislunar SDA work in the open literature includes optimization frameworks specifically for optical-sensor cislunar SDA.
Earth–Moon one-way light time is only ~1.3 seconds at average distance; in most SA workflows the dominant delays are scheduling, downlink windows, and analyst decision cycles.
DSN techniques (range/Doppler/VLBI-family tracking) are the bedrock of deep-space navigation.

A track can be promoted from “candidate” to “cataloged” when association likelihood is high and the OD residual distribution is consistent with sensor noise (e.g., optical residual RMS near expected arcsec level).
Oracle-style onboard processing is explicitly intended to reduce downlink volume and potentially reduce latency pressures for cislunar space-based sensors.
For conjunction product exchange, emit CCSDS CDMs (or trajectory-covariance variants like TraCSS specifications where applicable).

NOAA’s Space Weather Prediction Center documents that ionospheric disturbance can degrade GPS positioning and can disrupt or attenuate radio communications (e.g., radio blackouts; scintillation impacts).

Operational mitigations include incorporating SWPC alerts into scheduler rules (e.g., increase reliance on optical when RF degraded; adjust measurement noise models during disturbed periods), and explicitly tracking “space weather state” as a contextual variable in anomaly detection.
The motivation for cislunar-placed sensors is explicitly tied to overcoming Earth-view exclusion zones and observation limitations.
Space Policy Directive-5 establishes cybersecurity principles for space systems and emphasizes risk-based, cybersecurity-informed engineering across space systems and their supporting infrastructure.
SPD-3 explicitly mentions encryption of satellite command-and-control links and data protection measures as part of operating in congested space environments.

Tasking, orbit products, and alert pipelines should be treated as critical infrastructure: requiring authenticated telemetry/tasking, least-privilege access, and continuous monitoring aligned with SPD-5 principles.
Standardized, validated interchange formats (CCSDS TDM/CDM) with strict schema validation and provenance metadata should be used to reduce data poisoning risk.

Recommendations are structured as scalable “effects-based” architectures (ground-only → hybrid → cislunar-placed) rather than procurement prescriptions due to unspecified budget and program authorities.

The report assumes alignment with existing U.S. STM policy definitions and interoperability goals, but does not assume any particular international data-sharing agreements beyond what is publicly described in strategy/policy documents.

Many national-security SDA sensors and tactics are not public; this report intentionally uses only publicly available descriptions (e.g., USSF fact sheets; OSTP strategy; CCSDS standards; peer-reviewed literature) and therefore may understate achievable performance for some actors.

prompt: Regarding the attitude control problem for UAVs, most open-source flight controllers currently implement cascaded PID control algorithms. However, a single set of PID controller parameters typically performs well only under specific flight conditions. In practical applications, UAVs operate across diverse flight states. What methods can be employed to enhance the actual control performance of PID algorithms, and how should PID parameters be optimally selected?
article:
Methods to Enhance PID-Based Attitude Control for UAVs and Optimal PID Parameter Selection Executive summary PID-based attitude control remains the dominant practical architecture in open-source and commercial UAV autopilots because it is computationally light, interpretable, and robust enough when paired with good estimation, filtering, actuator modeling, and safety constraints. PX4 and ArduPilot both implement cascaded control in which an outer attitude (angle) controller generates rate setpoints and an inner angular-rate controller (PID or “K‑PID”) generates torque/motor commands; PX4 documents this as a “standard cascaded control architecture” with EKF-based state estimates, while ArduPilot explicitly describes the “P (angle error → desired rate) + PID (rate error → motor command)” pattern and runs it at high rates (e.g., 400 Hz on Pixhawk-class hardware). [1] “Optimal” PID gains are not universal constants: the effective plant seen by the controller varies with battery voltage sag, motor/ESC thrust nonlinearity, airspeed (fixed-wing), payload mass/inertia changes, flexible frames, and actuator saturations/rate limits. Modern “best practice” therefore is less about a single perfect triplet (P/I/D) and more about a layered strategy: - first get mechanics + vibrations + filtering + thrust model/linearization correct (otherwise any gain optimization is fragile), - tune the inner rate loop for fast, well-damped response (step-response matching is a common workflow), - tune the outer attitude loop to be slower than the rate loop and to provide the desired “feel,” - then expand performance across the envelope using gain scheduling (airspeed, voltage, payload), robust tuning criteria (margins / H∞ bounds), and—where justified—online adaptation (self-tuning, MRAC/L1, gain compression), always with safety guardrails (anti-windup, saturation awareness, reversion logic). [2] Open-source stacks already include several “PID enhancement” mechanisms that are worth treating as first-class design elements: ArduPilot’s PID library includes integrator limiting/windup protection, target/error/derivative filtering, slew-rate limiting, and PD sum limiting, and ArduPilot offers battery-voltage gain scaling explicitly aimed at preserving attitude response. [3] PX4’s multicopter rate control includes integrator limits, feedforward gain, and an anti-windup path informed by saturation status from control allocation; PX4 fixed-wing adds gain compression, described as an automatic reduction of angular-rate PID gains when oscillations are detected. [4] Baseline cascaded PID attitude control in open-source flight controllers What “cascaded PID attitude control” looks like in practice ArduPilot documents its Copter attitude control as: a P controller converts angle error into a desired rotation rate, followed by a PID controller converting rate error into a high-level motor command, executed at high rate (e.g., “400hz on Pixhawk”). [5] ArduPilot also emphasizes a model-following architecture (pilot/autopilot commands → demanded attitude or axial rate; controllers drive the vehicle to that demand) and explicitly describes a feedforward path in the rate controller plus P/I/D feedback terms and integrator output limits. [6] PX4 similarly documents a standard cascaded control architecture and provides controller diagrams stating: - estimates come from EKF2, - the multicopter angular-rate controller is “K‑PID,” with integral authority limited to prevent windup, outputs limited by control allocation, and derivative filtering in the IMU pipeline, - the attitude controller uses quaternions and saturates its rate commands. [7] A simplified (platform-agnostic) view of the cascade is: flowchart LR CMD[Attitude command
roll/pitch/yaw or rates] --> OUTER[Outer attitude loop
(typically P or nonlinear attitude law)] OUTER --> RSP[Body rate setpoint p,q,r
+ optional accel setpoints] RSP --> INNER[Inner rate loop (PID/K-PID)
+ feedforward] IMU[Gyro / IMU pipeline
calibration + notch + LPF] --> EST[State estimate
(EKF/attitude estimator)] EST --> OUTER EST --> INNER INNER --> SAT[Saturation + allocation-aware anti-windup
+ limits] SAT --> ACT[Control allocation / mixing
motors or control surfaces] ACT --> PLANT[UAV rotational dynamics
+ actuator dynamics + disturbances] PLANT --> IMU “PID performance” is inseparable from estimation, filtering, and allocation Both stacks explicitly integrate anti-windup and saturation-awareness in the rate loop. PX4’s multicopter rate-control code shows: integrator limits are set, feedforward is configured, and saturation status from control allocation is passed into the controller (with a note that sending “unallocated” values could improve anti-windup). [8] ArduPilot’s PID implementation includes an explicit limit flag that prevents integrator growth when limited, constrains integrator magnitude, and applies slew-rate limiting and PD sum limiting to reduce high-frequency oscillations caused by excessive gain. [9] This matters for “practical flight conditions” because the dominant failure modes during aggressive tuning are often not “PID theory mistakes” but: actuator saturation at high thrust/tilt, nonlinear thrust response, vibration-driven phase lag, and sensor noise amplification—each of which open-source documentation flags as real-world issues to manage before expecting an autotuner or optimizer to work reliably. [10] Method families to enhance PID attitude control and optimize gain selection The table below is an at-a-glance comparator; detailed method notes follow. Method family (examples) Core principle Implementation complexity Compute cost (embedded) Required sensors/models Stability / robustness guarantees Best fit: small multirotors vs fixed-wing Gain scheduling (airspeed, voltage, payload, altitude, thrust) Switch/scale gains and/or limits as operating point changes Low–Medium Low Scheduling variables (airspeed, voltage/current, payload estimate) Depends on design; can preserve margins if schedules are smooth, bounded, and validated Both; airspeed scheduling is especially central for fixed-wing [11] Nonlinear compensation around PID (feedforward, thrust linearization, limiters, anti-windup) Reduce effective nonlinearity/limits that “break” linear PID assumptions Low–Medium Low Often none beyond IMU; thrust linearization needs thrust model Improves practical robustness; not a formal guarantee unless analyzed Both, especially multirotors with thrust nonlinearity and saturation [12] Autotuning in the flight stack (PX4 AutoTune, ArduPilot AutoTune/QuikTune) Inject maneuvers/excitation and compute gains targeting desired response Medium Low–Medium Reliable IMU + stable baseline; good vibration environment Usually procedural safety, not formal proof; PX4 includes explicit apply/revert safeguards and warnings Both; must be conservative for large aircraft / flexible frames [13] Robust PID tuning (loop shaping, H∞-bounded PID sets) Choose PID gains to meet margins or H∞ bounds; often frequency-domain design Medium–High Low (once gains computed), Medium if online Plant model or identified FRF; delay estimates Stronger design-time robustness arguments via margins/H∞ bounds Both; common on fixed-wing & larger multirotors where modelling/ID is feasible [14] Self-tuning PID (RLS/least squares plant ID → retune) Identify simplified model online and re-compute PID parameters Medium–High Medium Excitation + model structure; sometimes airspeed/throttle Stability depends on ID quality + supervisory logic Multirotors and fixed-wing where conditions vary slowly; needs careful safeguards [15] LMS / gradient adaptive PID Update gains by minimizing instantaneous tracking error cost Medium Low–Medium Minimal model; needs filtered signals Typically boundedness with constraints; sensitive to noise/saturation Small multirotors potentially, but noise makes naïve LMS risky without heavy filtering/limits [16] MRAC augmentation Force plant to track a desired reference model via adaptive law High Medium Reference model + measured states; sufficient excitation assumptions Classical MRAC stability under matching conditions; robust modifications exist More common in research/prototypes than mainstream open-source autopilot defaults [17] L1 adaptive augmentation MRAC-like adaptation with low-pass filtering to separate robustness from fast adaptation High Medium Model structure + filter design; measured states Explicit “guaranteed transient performance” claims in L1 literature Promising for aggressive multirotors under uncertainty; integration complexity is high [18] Kalman-filter-based parameter estimation feeding scheduling/adaptation Estimate mass/inertia/disturbances → adjust gains/FF/limits Medium–High Medium EKF/UKF + selected measurement signals Depends on estimator observability; can improve robustness if bounded Generally practical when you already run EKF and can log/validate innovations [19] Bayesian optimization / safe BO for gain search Treat tuning as expensive black-box optimization with safety constraints High (tooling), Medium (offline) Offline high; online medium if few trials Experiment evaluation metric; safety detector/constraints “Safe” BO methods explicitly aim to avoid unsafe gains Strong for labs and production tuning pipelines; risky for ad-hoc field tuning [20] Genetic algorithms / PSO / DE optimization Search gain space for objective (rise time, IAE, overshoot, etc.) Medium–High Offline high Simulation or test harness, objective function No inherent stability guarantee unless constrained Good offline; can seed autotune; less suited for direct in-flight search [21] ML approaches (RL gain tuning; NN controllers) Learn gains or policy from data/simulation; may replace PID cascade High Medium–High Training environment and domain randomization; runtime may need NN inference Guarantees are weak unless wrapped in safety constraints Best as research/experimental or with strict safety layers; not “drop-in” for safety-critical ops [22] Method notes with the requested attributes Gain scheduling by flight state (throttle, airspeed, altitude/air density, payload, battery voltage) Principle. Gains (and sometimes limits/filters) are scaled as a function of operating conditions so the effective closed-loop bandwidth and damping remain approximately constant across the flight envelope. Airspeed is fundamental for fixed-wing because control-surface effectiveness scales strongly with dynamic pressure. ArduPilot explicitly states its fixed-wing stabilization gains are “automatically scaled with airspeed” and that this scaling can even use an estimated airspeed when no airspeed sensor is present. [23] PX4 fixed-wing provides dedicated guidance on compensating for changes in weight and air density (via TECS tuning), and exposes “gain compression” for rate-loop oscillation suppression. [24] Implementation complexity. Low if schedules are simple scalars (e.g., voltage ratio, airspeed ratio) and only apply to a few gains. Medium if using multi-dimensional maps (airspeed × altitude × configuration) or if you schedule inner-loop bandwidth and re-derive gains. Computational cost. Usually negligible (a few multiplies per loop). Required sensors/models. - Airspeed scheduling: ideally an airspeed sensor, though ArduPilot notes it can estimate airspeed using “GPS velocity, position changes, and IMU accelerations.” [23] - Voltage scheduling: requires voltage monitoring; ArduPilot’s voltage scaling explicitly requires a power module/voltage monitor. [25] - Payload scheduling: needs either a known payload state or an estimate (or conservative “worst case” tuning). ArduPilot gives an explicit payload scaling heuristic for acceleration limits based on min/max takeoff weight. [26] - Altitude/air density: typically requires altitude estimate (baro/GNSS) and an atmosphere model; PX4 TECS “weight and altitude tuning” is specifically aimed at compensating for weight and air density changes. [27] Stability/robustness guarantees. Not automatic. In practice you aim for: bounded schedules, smooth transitions, and validation that gain/phase margins remain acceptable at worst-case operating points. The main advantage is predictability: schedules are explicit and reviewable. Pros. Very practical; aligns with known physics (airspeed, voltage); inexpensive and explainable. Cons. Requires careful validation across the envelope; if schedule variables are noisy or biased, scheduling can inject gain noise. Suitability. - Small multirotors: very useful for voltage and thrust nonlinearity compensation; less common to schedule by airspeed (no airspeed sensor) but scheduling by payload/configuration can help (e.g., camera on/off). ArduPilot explicitly provides voltage scaling for attitude gains. [25] - Fixed-wing: airspeed scheduling is often essential, and ArduPilot warns that changing the center speed (SCALING_SPEED) after tuning can “corrupt the tune” and cause instability. [28] Nonlinear compensation and “PID hardening” (feedforward, anti-windup, deadband, rate limiting, command shaping) Principle. Keep the PID operating in a regime where its linear assumptions are “approximately true,” and prevent integrator/derivative terms from creating instability when actuators saturate or signals are noisy. Implementation complexity. Low–Medium. Many elements already exist in open-source stacks: - ArduPilot’s PID library includes IMAX limiting, integrator windup protection when limit is active, and explicit slew-rate limiting that can reduce P+D gain under high-frequency oscillations, plus PD sum limiting. [9] - ArduPilot attitude-control code supports shaping rate/accel targets with configured limits/time constants (“rate feedforward shaping”). [29] - PX4 rate control explicitly limits integral authority and uses control allocation saturation status for anti-windup. [30] Computational cost. Low for limiters and shaping; moderate if adding advanced filtering or frequency-adaptive notch filters (see below). Required sensors/models. - Feedforward requires a meaningful setpoint (desired rate/accel). Both ArduPilot and PX4 incorporate feedforward concepts in their rate control implementations. [31] - Thrust linearization uses a thrust model and (ideally) thrust stand/log data; see “Scheduling maps” section. Stability/robustness guarantees. These features typically provide practical robustness more than formal proof, but anti-windup and saturation awareness directly address known nonlinearities that can destabilize PID loops. Pros. Often the highest ROI improvements; reduces crash risk; improves repeatability of tuning. Cons. Too much filtering adds phase lag (reducing stability margins). PX4 log analysis guidance explicitly notes that good vibration characteristics allow increasing filter cutoffs to reduce control latency, and warns not to fly with excessively high vibration levels. [32] Suitability. Essential for both multirotors and fixed-wing; multirotors especially benefit because high thrust/tilt maneuvers frequently hit actuator limits and amplifies vibration/noise paths. Autotuning tools within PX4 and ArduPilot Principle. Use controlled excitation/step tests to identify the vehicle response and compute gains that achieve a target closed-loop behavior (often a desired rise time and damping). Implementation complexity. - For users, complexity is low (guided procedure). - For developers, medium (system identification + design + safety checks). Computational cost. Low–Medium, but bounded (the tuning is typically occasional, not continuous). Required sensors/models. A stable baseline tune, reliable gyro/attitude estimates, and sufficiently low vibration/noise. ArduPilot warns AutoTune can fail due to “strong wind,” “high levels of gyro noise,” incorrect thrust expo (nonlinear ESC response), flexible frames, etc. [33] PX4 autotuning similarly notes that unmodeled effects like delay, saturation, slew-rate, and airframe flexibility can make loop gain too high after tuning. [34] Stability/robustness guarantees. - Not a mathematical guarantee; rather, safety comes from conservative procedures and guardrails. - PX4 explicitly warns that applying gains in air is dangerous because “there is no guarantee that those new gains will be able to stabilize the drone properly.” [35] Pros. - Fast path to “good enough” gains; scales across airframes. - Built-in to common tooling: QGroundControl recommends auto-tuning for supported vehicle types and frames. [36] Cons. - Can be misled by wind, vibration, actuator nonlinearity, and misconfigured thrust models. - Still requires pilot readiness to abort. Suitability. - Small multirotors: widely used; ensure thrust model and vibration setup are good first. PX4 recommends tuning around hover thrust and using thrust curve compensation for high-thrust nonlinearities/oscillations. [37] - Fixed-wing: feasible but requires trimming and safe manual piloting skill (PX4 fixed-wing tuning guide explicitly requires the pilot be able to fly/land in manual override). [38] Notable open-source implementations. - ArduPilot AutoTune: tunes “Stabilize P, Rate P and D, and maximum rotational accelerations,” requires the vehicle be basically flyable in AltHold, and includes strong safety warnings. [33] - ArduPilot QuikTune (Lua): “slowly increases the relevant gains until it detects an oscillation,” then “reduces the gains by 60%” and proceeds. [39] - PX4 AutoTune: exposes system-ID and response targets (e.g., desired rise time, injected signal amplitude), and parameters explicitly formalize these concepts. [40] Robust PID tuning (loop shaping, H∞-inspired PID selection) Principle. Choose PID gains to meet robustness criteria explicitly—often by shaping the open-loop frequency response to achieve desired crossover frequency and phase margin, or by constraining the closed-loop sensitivity/error transfer function using H∞ bounds. The PID-2018 paper “PID controller design with an H∞ criterion” frames the problem as computing the stabilizing set of PI/PID controllers that achieve an H∞ norm bound and explicitly connects this design to gain/phase margin concepts. [41] Implementation complexity. Medium–High: you need a plant model or an identified frequency response (FRF), and a robust tuning workflow. In a UAV context, this is often paired with system identification from logs and then offline controller synthesis. Computational cost. Typically: - Offline: moderate–high (optimization / set computation). - Online: low (once fixed gains are loaded). Required sensors/models. Either: - a physics model (inertia, actuator model, aerodynamic derivatives), or - an identified FRF (e.g., from step/chirp tests), plus delay estimates. Stability/robustness guarantees. Stronger than ad-hoc tuning in the sense that robustness objectives are explicit and reviewable (phase margin / H∞ bound). However, guarantees are only as good as the model/ID quality and how well the real system matches assumptions (especially delays, saturations, and flexible modes—exactly the effects PX4 notes can break model-based assumptions after autotune). [42] Pros. Predictable margins; good for certification-minded workflows; often scales well to fixed-wing and larger multirotors where modeling effort is justified. Cons. Requires engineering infrastructure (ID tools, frequency-domain analysis). Unmodeled flexible modes can still bite. Suitability. - Small multirotors: beneficial but must be vibration-aware; higher controller bandwidth is limited by IMU noise and actuator dynamics. - Fixed-wing: often an excellent fit due to clearer frequency separation between rigid-body dynamics and servo/airframe modes, and because airspeed scheduling can be integrated into the model. Self-tuning PID and LMS-based adaptive PID Principle. - Self-tuning PID commonly means: estimate a simplified plant model online (often with least squares / RLS) and recompute PID parameters to match a reference model or optimize a criterion. - LMS adaptive PID updates PID gains via a gradient-like rule that minimizes a tracking error cost; LMS-based “self-learning PID” architectures explicitly adapt each gain using LMS. [16] Implementation complexity. Medium–High, because you must: 1) pick an identification model structure, 2) ensure persistent/adequate excitation (or accept slow/biased convergence), 3) enforce hard constraints on gains and integrator states, 4) implement supervisory logic to revert on instability. Computational cost. Medium but often feasible on modern flight controllers (matrix operations can be small because models are low order). Required sensors/models. Primarily closed-loop measurements (rates, commands). For best results you need well-filtered signals and careful accounting for saturation. Stability/robustness guarantees. Generally weaker than MRAC/L1 unless you add robustification and supervisory constraints. Practical self-tuning schemes often need explicit “safe sets” or stability monitors. Pros. - Adapts to slow changes (battery, payload, prop wear). - Can be designed to be “PID-like” and thus easier to integrate into existing stacks. Cons. - Sensitive to noise and unmodeled dynamics. - Online identification under closed-loop operation can become ill-conditioned without sufficient excitation, and “learning” during aggressive maneuvers is risky. Suitability. - Small multirotors: possible but must be conservative; use extensive filtering and bounded adaptation. - Fixed-wing: attractive when airspeed and flight conditions change slowly and excitation can be injected safely. Online parameter adaptation via MRAC and L1 adaptive control Principle. - MRAC: adapt controller parameters so the plant output follows a chosen reference model. NASA work on MRAC modifications targets “guaranteed transient performance” by modifying the reference model using tracking error feedback. [43] - L1 adaptive: an MRAC-derived architecture that uses filtering and small-gain arguments to allow fast adaptation while maintaining robustness; the foundational L1 paper emphasizes “guaranteed transient performance,” and the L1 book description similarly highlights robustness with fast adaptation. [18] Implementation complexity. High: requires careful design of reference models, filters, adaptation laws, projection/dead-zone/sigma-modification, and a thorough saturation/actuator model. Robust MRAC modifications (dead-zone, σ-modification, e-modification, projection) are explicitly discussed in adaptive control literature as necessary for realistic noisy environments. [44] Computational cost. Medium: typically a parameter update law each cycle plus filtering. Often feasible, but integration complexity is the real barrier. Required sensors/models. Usually requires full state (or good estimates), and a reliable mapping from control output to plant response. For attitude control this means reliable rates and attitude estimates (IMU+EKF). Stability/robustness guarantees. - MRAC: classic Lyapunov-stability results depend on matching conditions and assumptions that are violated by saturation and unmodeled dynamics; robust modifications help ensure boundedness. [44] - L1: literature explicitly claims transient performance and robustness properties under architecture assumptions. [18] Pros. - Potentially strong robustness to parametric uncertainty and payload changes. - Can outperform fixed-gain PID in aggressive maneuvers when model uncertainty is high. Cons. - High integration and validation burden. - Hard to certify for safety without extensive proof/testing infrastructure. - Poorly designed adaptation can destabilize due to noise, delay, and saturations. Suitability. - Small multirotors: relevant for high-performance/agile platforms and research; requires strong safety overlays. - Fixed-wing: used in aerospace research but less common in maker/open-source default stacks due to complexity. Kalman-filter-based estimation feeding gain adaptation/scheduling Principle. Use EKF/UKF to estimate time-varying parameters (e.g., inertia, thrust scale, disturbance torques) and then update scheduling variables or feedforward terms. PX4 already integrates a “hover thrust estimator” parameter group, including an innovation-gated estimator with configurable process noise and explicit range around MPC_THR_HOVER; it recommends setting the range larger when the vehicle operates with varying conditions like differently sized payloads. [45] Implementation complexity. Medium–High: you must: - define a parameterized model, - ensure observability under typical maneuvers, - gate innovations to avoid divergence. Computational cost. Medium (Kalman update), often feasible (and many stacks already run EKF for state estimation). Required sensors/models. Sufficient excitation plus measurement signals relevant to the parameter. For example, hover thrust estimation uses acceleration-related innovation gating parameters and velocity thresholds. [45] Stability/robustness guarantees. Estimation stability is separate from control stability; overall robustness improves when the estimator is bounded and used in a bounded scheduling path. Pros. - Fits naturally into the “estimation-first” architecture of UAV autopilots. - Provides interpretable physical parameters (hover thrust, thrust scaling). Cons. - Parameter observability can be poor in steady hover with low excitation. - Bad estimates can degrade performance if not bounded. Suitability. Strong fit for both categories; especially useful for multirotors carrying variable payloads and for fixed-wing energy management (weight/air density compensation). PX4 gain compression (adaptive robustness mechanism for fixed-wing) Principle. Automatically reduce angular-rate PID gains if oscillations are detected; this is effectively a safety-oriented adaptive mechanism that only attenuates gains. PX4 documents gain compression as monitoring angular-rate controller output through a band-pass filter, keeping PID gains unchanged when no oscillations are present, never increasing them beyond nominal values, and bounding them by a minimum limit. [46] Implementation complexity. Medium (detection filter + gain attenuation logic), but conceptually simpler than full MRAC/L1. Computational cost. Low. Required sensors/models. Uses internal controller signals (rate controller output) rather than requiring a plant model. [46] Stability/robustness guarantees. Not a formal proof, but the “only reduce gains” design is aligned with safety: it aims to avoid oscillatory instability rather than chase performance. Pros. High practical value for fixed-wing, where oscillations can arise from flexible modes or poorly modeled dynamics. Cons. It is reactive: it reduces performance when oscillations are detected, and it doesn’t directly optimize nominal performance. Suitability. Particularly strong for fixed-wing; conceptually also applicable to multirotors, but PX4 documents it specifically for fixed-wing. [46] Optimization-based PID tuning (GA/PSO/DE) and Bayesian optimization Principle. - GA/PSO/DE: solve an objective (time-domain metrics like overshoot/settling or integral error) by sampling gain sets. Quadrotor PID tuning with PSO and DE variants is frequently studied in the literature. [21] - Bayesian optimization (BO): model the mapping from gains → performance with a Gaussian process and choose the next test efficiently; safe variants explicitly manage risk. Safe controller optimization for quadrotors with Gaussian processes is a prominent example of safety-aware controller tuning. [47] A closely related “safety-aware cascade controller tuning” paper uses constrained BO specifically for PID cascade tuning and includes stability constraints learned from data. [48] Implementation complexity. High if done online on hardware (tooling, safety, automation). Medium if done offline in simulation and then validated. Computational cost. Mostly offline; online is feasible only if you can tolerate few trials and have strong abort logic. Required sensors/models. - Black-box methods often require no explicit model, but require reliable evaluation metrics and repeatable test maneuvers. Stability/robustness guarantees. - Unconstrained search has no guarantee. - Safe BO methods explicitly aim to avoid unsafe parameterizations during experiments. [20] Pros. - Can produce high-quality tuning for bespoke platforms. - Can encode multi-objective trade-offs (tracking vs energy vs noise). Cons. - Risky on real vehicles without safety constraints. - Performance is only as good as the objective definition and test scenario coverage. Suitability. - Small multirotors: best when you can run extensive simulation (SITL) and validate incrementally. - Fixed-wing: strong candidate for manufacturer tuning pipelines, where repeatable test profiles exist. Machine-learning approaches (RL, supervised regression, neural controllers) Principle. - RL for PID tuning frames gain selection as a sequential decision/optimization under constraints; example work formulates PID tuning as an RL task with constraints. [49] - Neural controllers can replace or augment PID; PX4 includes an experimental “MC Neural Networks Control” module (mc_nn_control) explicitly intended to experiment with pre-trained neural networks running on the flight controller (TensorFlow Lite Micro integration). [50] A recent arXiv paper describes an open-sourced PX4 neural-network controller framework that can substitute the control cascade and output motor RPM setpoints. [51] Implementation complexity. High. Beyond training, the hard part is safety assurance: bounding outputs, handling unseen conditions, and certification-level validation. Computational cost. Medium–High at runtime depending on network size; training cost is high but offline. Required sensors/models. Often relies on simulation and domain randomization; runtime still needs state estimates and reliable sensors. Stability/robustness guarantees. Typically weak unless combined with formal safety layers or safe learning frameworks; “experimental” labels in official docs should be taken seriously. [52] Pros. Potential to handle complex nonlinearities and morphologies; can adapt across platforms if trained appropriately. Cons. Hard to validate; can fail catastrophically outside training distribution. Suitability. Best as research/experimental or as offline tools that output PID schedules, rather than as direct online controllers, unless strict safety wrappers are used. Practical tuning and validation workflows This section synthesizes open-source recommended workflows (PX4/QGC and ArduPilot) into a practical, safety-oriented pipeline. Assumption: no specific UAV platform is given, so the workflow is described generically and should be adapted to your airframe, actuator bandwidth, and mission envelope. Pre-tuning prerequisites that dominate success 1) Tune in the most agile/“hardest” configuration first. ArduPilot recommends the initial tune be done in the aircraft’s “most agile configuration,” generally minimum takeoff weight with fully charged batteries. [53] 2) Fix vibration and filtering before “gain hunting.” - PX4 Flight Review warns you should not fly with extremely high vibration levels and provides frequency-domain tools (FFT / spectral density) to diagnose and guide filter choices; it even notes that excellent vibration characteristics allow increasing filter cutoffs to reduce latency. [32] - ArduPilot provides notch-filter guidance and in-flight FFT tooling (with cautions that in-flight FFT can lag and be inferior to a properly configured throttle-based notch filter). [54] 3) Linearize thrust / map command-to-thrust as early as possible. PX4 explicitly links high-thrust oscillations to thrust non-linearities and recommends thrust curve compensation when tuning around hover is insufficient. [55] ArduPilot notes incorrect motor thrust expo can prevent AutoTune from providing a good tune. [33] Manual tuning workflow aligned with PX4 and ArduPilot guidance A conservative workflow that maps tightly to PX4’s step-response guidance and ArduPilot’s tuning process is: Step: Establish safe baseline and logging - PX4: default gains may be low and must be increased carefully; it recommends increasing gains slowly (e.g., 20–30% steps, then 5–10% for fine tuning) and landing before changing parameters. [56] - Enable high-rate logging where available (PX4 explicitly suggests enabling a high-rate logging profile for evaluating rate/attitude tracking). [55] Step: Tune inner rate loop first (roll, pitch, yaw) - Use a step-response procedure: PX4’s basic tuning guide instructs users to rapidly move the stick full range and observe the step response; the goal is for the “Response curve to match the Setpoint curve as closely as possible (fast response without overshoots).” [57] - Use classic heuristics consistent with PX4 guidance: - Increase P for responsiveness; reduce if overshoot/oscillation occurs. - Increase D to dampen overshoot/oscillation, but only as much as needed because it amplifies noise and can cause hot motors. - Increase I to reduce steady-state error (e.g., in wind); too high can cause slow oscillations. [57] Step: Tune outer attitude loop - Ensure the attitude loop remains slower than the rate loop (PX4 troubleshooting explicitly links slow oscillations on large platforms to attitude loop being too fast relative to rate loop). [58] - In many architectures (including PX4 multicopter attitude), the primary tuning knob is attitude P. [59] Step: Validate across thrust / aggressiveness - PX4 explicitly advises tuning around hover thrust and using thrust curve compensation for higher thrust non-linearities or high-thrust oscillations. [37] - ArduPilot suggests input shaping and acceleration limits to achieve desired “feel,” and provides payload scaling guidance for acceleration limits. [60] Autotuning workflows in open-source practice PX4 autotune readiness test (safety gate). QGroundControl’s PX4 tuning guide says auto-tuning is performed in flight and the vehicle must first demonstrate it can stabilize within ~2 oscillations under progressively larger tilt maneuvers (roll and pitch). [61] PX4 autotune safety and application policy. - PX4 parameter reference documents an explicit apply policy that warns applying gains in air is dangerous (no guarantee of stability), and provides an option to apply only after disarm. [35] ArduPilot AutoTune prerequisites and failure modes. - ArduPilot AutoTune requires the copter be “basically flyable” before use and warns it can generate unflyable gains and crash the vehicle; it highlights wind, gyro noise, and thrust nonlinearity as key failure contributors. [33] - ArduPilot’s overall tuning process explicitly recommends setting up notch filters and (optionally) QuikTune before attempting AutoTune. [62] QuikTune as a supervised “gain ramp.” ArduPilot’s QuikTune uses a Lua script that ramps gains until oscillation is detected, then reduces gains by 60% and continues. [39] This is conceptually similar to a cautious “search for critical gain” process, but implemented with guardrails. Recommended tuning metrics and what they diagnose Below are metrics commonly used in UAV attitude tuning, mapped to the practical failure modes open-source tooling emphasizes. Metric How to measure in practice What it tells you Rise time / closed-loop “snappiness” Step response in QGC plots (setpoint vs response) for rate loops [57] Bandwidth proxy; too fast risks noise amplification and saturation Overshoot and oscillation count Step response; QGC autotune pre-test expects stabilization within ~2 oscillations [61] Damping ratio / phase margin proxy Steady-state rate error under disturbance Hover in wind; monitor if response reaches setpoint (PX4 notes low I may never reach setpoint in wind) [57] Integral adequacy and bias rejection Disturbance rejection vs actuator limits Examine controller saturation flags and recovery; PX4 rate controller passes saturation status for anti-windup [63] Whether anti-windup/limits are configured and whether gains demand unrealistic torques Noise sensitivity / latency PSD/FFT of accelerometer/gyro; PX4 Flight Review provides frequency-domain plots and warns against high vibration [32] Whether D-term and filter cutoffs are safe Robustness across battery depletion Track behavior at full vs low voltage; ArduPilot supports voltage scaling of attitude gains [25] Need for voltage compensation / thrust model updates Test procedures: step tests, frequency sweeps, HIL, flight tests Step tests (in flight). PX4’s basic tuning guide formalizes this by instructing roll stick full-range motions and inspecting setpoint/response curves; this is a direct and practical method to estimate damping and rise time. [57] Frequency sweep / system identification. In UAV practice you can use: - dedicated system identification maneuvers (often part of autotune sequences), - log-based spectral analysis for vibration and flexible modes (PX4 Flight Review; ArduPilot FFT tools). [64] If you implement explicit chirp injection for controller design, constrain amplitudes and add abort logic similar to autotune safety patterns. Hardware-in-the-loop (HITL/HIL). - PX4 defines HITL as running normal PX4 firmware on real flight controller hardware with an external simulator; it highlights the benefit of testing most actual flight code on real hardware. [65] - ArduPilot provides “Simulation on Hardware,” similar to HIL in concept, using the board to run both flight code and simulation. [66] Safety considerations (strongly recommended). - Use calm-weather, open-area tuning conditions; ArduPilot explicitly recommends low wind and normal weather for tuning flights, and warns the first takeoff is particularly dangerous. [67] - Be ready to abort autotuning: QGroundControl recommends being ready to abort by moving sticks. [61] - Treat “apply gains in air” features as hazardous unless you have a reversion test (PX4 explicitly warns against in-air application). [35] - Avoid tuning on a high-vibration airframe; PX4 warns not to fly with unsafe vibration, and ArduPilot notes gyro noise and flexible mounts can break autotune. [68] Scheduling maps and parameter ranges from open-source practice This section provides concrete, source-backed examples of scheduling knobs and “maps” that exist in open-source stacks. These are not universal best values; they are starting points and safe ranges recorded in documentation. Thrust nonlinearity compensation (multirotors) Stack Parameter(s) What it does Practical ranges / notes PX4 THR_MDL_FAC Models nonlinear relationship between motor control signal and static thrust: rel_thrust = factor * rel_signal^2 + (1-factor) * rel_signal. [69] PX4 docs commonly recommend typical factors ~0.3–0.5 for PWM-driven systems (tune as needed); older PX4 guidance states typical values are between 0.3 and 0.5. [70] PX4 “Thrust curve value” guidance When control-signal-to-thrust is not linear, optimal hover tuning may not hold at higher thrust; use thrust curve value to compensate. [71] PX4 basic guide suggests 0.3 as a good default for PWM controllers and 1 for RPM-based controllers (quadratic thrust curve). [72] ArduPilot MOT_THST_EXPO Motor thrust scaling to compensate ESC/motor thrust nonlinearity; 0 is linear, 1 is very curved; default documented as 0.65. [73] ArduPilot warns some ESCs already linearize; default 0.65 can cause stability issues and then 0–0.2 may be required. [73] Battery-voltage-based gain scaling and thrust scaling Stack Parameter(s) Scheduling principle Practical notes ArduPilot Copter MOT_BAT_VOLT_MAX, MOT_BAT_VOLT_MIN Increase roll/pitch/yaw control gains as voltage drops to maintain attitude response; set VOLT_MAX to full-charge voltage, VOLT_MIN to minimum experienced voltage (often failsafe voltage). [25] ArduPilot explicitly states “Gains will be scaled to attempt to maintain the attitude control response seen at full charge.” [25] PX4 Hover thrust estimator (HTE_*) + MPC_THR_HOVER Estimate hover thrust and use it as a baseline for altitude control; bound the estimate around MPC_THR_HOVER. [74] HTE_THR_RANGE example: 0.2 around MPC_THR_HOVER=0.5 gives [0.3, 0.7]; PX4 suggests larger range for varying conditions like differently sized payloads. [45] Airspeed-based scheduling (fixed-wing attitude/rate loops) Stack Parameter(s) Scheduling principle Critical caution ArduPilot Plane AIRSPEED_MIN, AIRSPEED_MAX, SCALING_SPEED Stabilization gains are automatically scaled with airspeed; ArduPilot may estimate airspeed from GPS/IMU when no sensor is present. [23] ArduPilot warns do not change SCALING_SPEED after tuning because it scales gains and can “corrupt the tune” and lead to instability. [28] PX4 Fixed-wing TECS “weight and altitude tuning” Provides methods to compensate for weight and air density changes; includes the concept of service ceiling for compensation. [27] Requires basic TECS tuning first; documentation emphasizes it is an advanced topic. [75] Payload / configuration scheduling via limits (a practical “safe adaptation”) ArduPilot provides a straightforward payload scaling heuristic for maximum angular acceleration limits (part of “feel” and aggressiveness control): for large directly mounted payloads, reduce ATC_ACC_*_MAX by multiplying by (min_TOW / max_TOW) for pitch/roll/yaw. [26] This is not “gain scheduling” of P/I/D directly, but it is often safer than gain scheduling because it limits commanded aggressiveness without changing inner-loop stability-critical gains. Autotune configuration ranges (PX4 example) PX4’s parameter reference documents important autotune knobs: - MC_AT_RISE_TIME: desired angular-rate closed-loop rise time (range 0.01–0.5 s; default shown as 0.14 s). [35] - MC_AT_SYSID_AMP: injected signal amplitude (0.1–6.0; default shown as 0.7). [35] - MC_AT_APPLY: controls when gains are applied; explicitly warns in-air application is dangerous. [35] Decision flowchart for selecting PID enhancement methods This decision flow is deliberately conservative: it biases toward changes that are explainable, testable, and already supported by open-source tooling before recommending high-complexity adaptation or ML. flowchart TD A[Start: Need better attitude performance or robustness] --> B{Baseline issues fixed?
mechanics, vibration, filtering, thrust model} B -- No --> B1[Fix vibration & filtering first
• PX4 FFT/PSD log review
• ArduPilot notch/FFT setup] --> A B -- Yes --> C{Vehicle type?} C -- Multirotor --> D{Performance degrades with thrust / battery?} C -- Fixed-wing --> E{Airspeed/weight/altitude vary significantly?} D -- Yes --> D1[Thrust model / linearization
PX4 THR_MDL_FAC, ArduPilot MOT_THST_EXPO] --> D2[Add scheduling: voltage scaling / hover thrust estimation] --> F D -- No --> F[Standard tuning: rate loop -> attitude loop
use step response & logs] E -- Yes --> E1[Airspeed-based scaling and TECS weight/altitude tuning
ArduPilot SCALING_SPEED; PX4 TECS advanced tuning] --> F E -- No --> F F --> G{Need automation?} G -- Yes --> H[Use built-in autotune safely
• PX4 autotune with apply-after-disarm
• ArduPilot QuikTune/AutoTune with calm-weather prerequisites] G -- No --> I[Manual fine-tuning + validation across envelope] H --> J{Still unstable or oscillatory in parts of envelope?} I --> J J -- Yes --> K[Add robustness layers
• anti-windup/limit tuning
• gain compression (FW PX4)
• conservative gain scheduling] J -- No --> Z[Done: document gains, test cases, and safety margins] K --> L{Need more robustness under large uncertainty?} L -- Yes --> M[Consider advanced methods (higher effort):
• robust loop-shaping / H∞ PID design
• self-tuning PID / constrained BO offline
• MRAC/L1 only with strict safety supervisor] L -- No --> Z Why this ordering is consistent with open-source guidance PX4 and ArduPilot both emphasize that nonlinearities, vibration/noise, and unmodeled effects can cause tuning/auto-tuning to fail or become unsafe. [76] ArduPilot’s tuning process explicitly includes notch filter setup and QuikTune/Manual tuning before AutoTune. [62] PX4 explicitly warns about in-air gain application because stability cannot be guaranteed. [35] Final note on “optimal” PID selection under real flight conditions For practical UAVs, “optimal PID” is most defensibly defined as: the gains (and schedules/limits) that meet mission-relevant tracking and disturbance rejection targets with acceptable stability margins across the validated flight envelope, rather than the gains that optimize a single hover-step response. Open-source guidance implicitly supports this: PX4 recommends tuning around hover thrust but adding thrust model compensation for nonlinearity at higher thrust, while ArduPilot recommends tuning in the most agile configuration and provides explicit mechanisms for voltage scaling and payload-aware limit scaling.
